figures.caption: Fig. 1 We present Composer, the first framework that supports explicit composition control, enabling composition transfer in image generation. From left to right: the reference image (Ref.), the result guided by spatial structure only (Struct. only), the result guided by color distribution only (Color only), and the overall composition transfer result (Both).

Fig. 5 Combined Composition Transfer. The generated result is guided by the spatial structure from one reference image (Struct. Ref.) and the color distribution from another reference image (Color Ref.), demonstrating the ability to integrate different composition elements.

Fig. 7 Qualitative Comparison between Composer and Baseline Methods. Composer denotes our framework with explicit composition planning, while ref. free denotes the implicit (reference-free) variant. Both IPAdapter and our method use the same reference image (Ref.), with the corresponding composition representations denoted as Struct. (spatial structure) and Color Dist. (color distribution).

Table 2 Quantitative Comparison Results. Composer denotes our framework with explicit composition planning, while Composer-reference free denotes the implicit (reference-free) variant. We report text-image alignment (CLIP Score [34], HPS v2 [40], ImageReward [41]), image quality (FID [39]), and aesthetic appeal (Aesthetic Score [22], HPS v2 [40], ImageReward [41]). Notably, IPAdapter uses the same composition planner of our framework to retrieve reference images.

Fig. 13 Qualitative comparison on SDXL. From left to right: prompt/condition, SD-v1.5 baseline, RPG-SDXL, retrieved reference, IP-Adapter-XL, extracted structure, extracted color distribution, and Composer-XL. Composer-XL better preserves compositional aesthetics while maintaining strong text consistency.

Fig. 6 Composition Manipulation. From left to right: the reference image (Ref.), the extracted composition representation (Condition), and the generated results under different textual themes with combined composition control.

Fig. 2 Overview of Composer framework, which consists of two main components: (1) Composition Control: Extracting semantic-agnostic composition representations, including spatial structure and color distribution, which are fed into the Union-ControlNet module to generate images that satisfy both the textual and composition conditions. (2) Composition Planning: Starting from an external aesthetic database, we obtain a set of candidate images through Semantic Filtering. Given some composition referring examples, we utilize the in-context learning capabilities of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) to perform composition-aware retrieval from the image set based on the given textual theme. Union-ControlNet is initially trained on paired text–image data to internalize explicit composition conditioning. It is then fine-tuned with a text-to-composition objective to acquire implicit composition planning; during inference, we perform composition planning to achieve text-to-image aesthetic enhancement.
